calendar.title: Chili Growing Calendar
calendar.subtitle: Step-by-step guide from seed to harvest with disease prevention

field.seedling1: Sow seeds in seed trays with fine, moist potting mix
field.transplanting1: Transplant when seedlings have 4-6 true leaves
field.transplanting2: Harden off plants for 5-7 days before transplanting
field.vegetative4: Mulch to retain moisture and suppress weeds
field.flowering1: Switch to potassium-rich fertilizer for fruit development
field.flowering2: Reduce nitrogen to prevent excessive foliage
field.flowering3: Monitor for fruit set — hand-pollinate if needed
field.flowering4: Watch for anthracnose on developing fruits
field.harvest1: Harvest when fruits reach desired color and firmness
field.harvest2: Pick every 3-5 days to encourage continued production
field.harvest3: Use clean, sharp scissors — don't pull fruits
field.harvest4: Store at 7-10°C for maximum shelf life

smallSpace.seedling1: Use seedling trays or small pots on your windowsill
smallSpace.seedling2: Place near a south-facing window or under LED grow light
smallSpace.seedling3: Use a heat mat if your apartment is cool
smallSpace.seedling4: Mist with a spray bottle — small pots dry fast
smallSpace.transplanting1: Move to 5-10L pots, one plant per pot
smallSpace.transplanting2: Use quality potting mix (cocopeat + perlite + compost)
smallSpace.transplanting3: Place pots on balcony or near the brightest window
smallSpace.transplanting4: Add slow-release fertilizer to the potting mix
smallSpace.vegetative1: Water when top 2cm of soil feels dry (check daily)
smallSpace.vegetative2: Use liquid fertilizer every 2 weeks at half strength
smallSpace.vegetative3: Use small bamboo stakes or plant clips for support
smallSpace.vegetative4: Rotate pots weekly for even light exposure
smallSpace.flowering2: Gently shake plants to help pollination indoors
smallSpace.flowering3: Ensure 6-8 hours of direct light or supplement with LEDs
smallSpace.flowering4: Watch for spider mites in dry indoor conditions
smallSpace.harvest1: Pick peppers when they reach full color
smallSpace.harvest2: Harvest regularly to encourage more fruit production
smallSpace.harvest3: Snip with scissors — avoid damaging the branch
smallSpace.harvest4: Dry extras on a sunny windowsill or dehydrator

disease.vegetative1: Cercospora leaf spot — apply fungicide if spotted
disease.flowering1: Anthracnose — the #1 chili threat, apply mancozeb preventively
disease.flowering2: TYLCV — watch for leaf curl, remove infected plants
disease.harvest2: Fruit rot — sort and remove damaged fruits immediately

guide.title: Urban Growing Guide
guide.subtitle: Everything you need to grow chili peppers in apartments, balconies, and small spaces

containerSelection.title: Container Selection
containerSelection.description: Choosing the right container is crucial for healthy chili plants in limited spaces.
containerSelection.tip1Desc: The ideal size for most chili varieties. Provides enough root space for healthy growth and fruit production. Use pots with drainage holes.
containerSelection.tip2Desc: Perfect for busy urban growers. Built-in water reservoir keeps soil consistently moist. Reduces watering frequency to once every 3-5 days.
containerSelection.tip3Desc: Lightweight, affordable, and breathable. Fabric grow bags promote air pruning of roots, leading to healthier root systems. Easy to store when not in use.

soilMedia.title: Soil & Growing Media
soilMedia.description: Container plants need a specially formulated potting mix — garden soil alone won't work.
soilMedia.tip1Desc: Mix 40% cocopeat, 30% perlite, and 30% compost. This creates a lightweight, well-draining mix that retains moisture without waterlogging.
soilMedia.tip2Desc: Garden soil compacts in containers, reducing drainage and air circulation. Always use a soilless potting mix for containers.
soilMedia.tip3Desc: Mix in slow-release fertilizer granules at planting time. Supplement with liquid fertilizer every 2 weeks during the growing season.

indoorLighting.title: Indoor Lighting
indoorLighting.description: Chili peppers need 6-8 hours of direct sunlight. Here's how to make it work indoors.
indoorLighting.tip1Desc: The best natural light source for indoor growing. Place plants directly on the windowsill or within 30cm of the glass for maximum light exposure.
indoorLighting.tip2Desc: Use full-spectrum LED grow lights if natural light is insufficient. Position 15-30cm above plants, run for 14-16 hours daily. Look for lights with 4000-6500K color temperature.
indoorLighting.tip3Desc: Chili peppers need at least 6 hours of direct light for fruiting. Seedlings benefit from 14-16 hours. Use a timer to maintain consistent light schedules.

wateringPots.title: Watering in Pots
wateringPots.description: Watering containers is different from garden beds. Here's how to get it right.
wateringPots.tip1Desc: Stick your finger 2cm into the soil. If it feels dry, water thoroughly until water drains from the bottom. In hot weather, you may need to water twice daily.
wateringPots.tip2Desc: Always use pots with drainage holes. Place a saucer underneath but empty it after 30 minutes to prevent root rot. Elevate pots on pot feet for better airflow.
wateringPots.tip3Desc: For holidays or busy schedules, use drip irrigation kits or self-watering stakes. A simple DIY option: invert a water bottle with small holes in the cap into the soil.

verticalGrowing.title: Vertical Growing
verticalGrowing.description: Maximize your growing space by thinking vertically — perfect for balconies and small patios.
verticalGrowing.tip1Desc: Use tiered plant stands or stackable planters to grow multiple plants in the footprint of one. Place sun-loving varieties on top tiers.
verticalGrowing.tip2Desc: Mount pocket planters or rail planters on balcony walls and railings. Great for compact chili varieties. Ensure they get adequate sunlight.
verticalGrowing.tip3Desc: Hang small chili varieties from ceiling hooks or balcony overhangs. Use lightweight pots with good drainage. Ornamental chili varieties look stunning in hanging baskets.

bestVarieties.title: Best Varieties for Small Spaces
bestVarieties.description: Not all chili varieties are suited for containers. These compact varieties thrive in pots.
bestVarieties.variety1Desc: Compact, prolific, and extremely hot. Grows to 30-45cm tall, perfect for pots. Produces hundreds of small, fiery peppers per plant.
bestVarieties.variety2Desc: Bushy and upright, ideal for containers. Grows 60-90cm tall. Produces abundant small peppers pointing upward — very ornamental.
bestVarieties.variety3Desc: Adaptable to container growing in 8-10L pots. Produces long, slender peppers great for drying. Moderate heat level, very productive.
bestVarieties.variety4Desc: Varieties like 'Black Pearl', 'NuMex Twilight', and 'Prairie Fire' are bred for compact growth. Edible, colorful, and perfect as decorative houseplants.
